protocols.title: Earthquake
steps: DROP to hands and knees immediately to prevent being knocked down.
Take COVER under a sturdy desk or table; if no table is nearby, cover your head and neck with your arms and crouch against an interior wall away from windows.
HOLD ON and protect your head/neck until shaking stops — do not attempt to run outside during shaking.
Once shaking stops, check yourself and others for injuries before moving.
Exit the building calmly using stairs, not elevators — inspect each step for cracks before putting weight on it.
Move to an open area away from buildings, power lines, and trees; expect aftershocks.
If trapped under debris, cover your mouth with clothing, tap on a pipe or wall to signal rescuers — shout only as a last resort to conserve air.
Do not re-enter any building until authorities declare it structurally safe.
avoid: Do not stand in a doorway — modern doorframes offer no special protection and you risk being hit by a swinging door.
Do not run outside during shaking — most injuries occur from falling debris when people try to flee.
Do not use elevators after an earthquake.
Do not light matches or candles until you have confirmed there is no gas leak.
Do not use tap water until local authorities confirm supply integrity.
Do not return to a damaged building to retrieve belongings.

protocols.title: House Fire
steps: Activate the nearest fire alarm if not already sounding, and shout 'FIRE' to alert all occupants.
Call emergency services (911 / local number) immediately — do this before attempting any action.
Feel closed doors with the back of your hand before opening; if hot, do not open — use an alternative exit route.
Stay low and crawl below the smoke line (cleaner air is near the floor) as you exit.
Exit through the nearest safe route; close — do not lock — doors behind you to slow fire spread.
Once outside, go directly to your pre-agreed meeting point and stay there.
If a person's clothing is on fire: STOP, DROP, and ROLL, or smother with a heavy blanket — do not allow them to run.
If you cannot escape, seal door gaps with towels or clothing, signal from a window, and call emergency services to give your exact location.
avoid: Do not use elevators — use stairs only.
Do not open a door without feeling it first — a hot door means fire is on the other side.
Do not waste time collecting belongings.
Do not re-enter a burning building for any reason.
Do not attempt to fight a fire beyond its incipient (small, contained) stage — evacuate instead.
Do not stand upright in a smoke-filled corridor.

protocols.title: Flood
steps: Monitor official alerts (weather radio, emergency broadcast, government apps) and heed evacuation orders immediately — do not wait.
If time permits before evacuation: move valuables and important documents to upper floors, turn off electricity at the breaker panel if safe to do so.
Evacuate on foot or vehicle along designated high-ground routes — avoid all flood-water crossings.
If caught in rising water indoors, move to the highest floor and, if necessary, the roof; signal for rescue from there.
If swept into moving water: float on your back, feet downstream, and work toward the bank at an angle with the current rather than fighting it directly.
Once floodwaters recede, do not return home until authorities declare it safe — waters may still be rising in your area.
Before re-entering a flooded structure, confirm utilities are off; document damage for insurance before cleanup begins.
Discard any food or water that has contacted floodwater — assume it is contaminated.
avoid: Do not drive or walk through floodwater — 15 cm (6 in) of moving water can knock an adult off their feet; 30 cm (12 in) can sweep away a vehicle.
Do not touch electrical equipment in wet areas.
Do not use tap water, well water, or stored water that may have been contaminated until tested.
Do not enter a building with structural damage visible — it may collapse.
Do not ignore evacuation orders to protect property.
Do not camp or park near streams, rivers, or drainage channels during heavy rainfall.

protocols.title: Active Threat (Run-Hide-Fight)
steps: RUN — If there is an accessible escape route, evacuate immediately; leave belongings behind; keep hands visible as you exit and follow law enforcement instructions.
Help others escape if you can do so safely, but do not let those who refuse to go slow your exit.
Call emergency services once you are safe; provide your location, number of people with you, and any information about the threat.
HIDE — If evacuation is not possible, get out of the threat's view: lock or barricade the door, turn off lights, silence all devices, and stay low and silent.
Move away from the door, position yourself behind solid cover (not just concealment), and do not open the door for anyone until law enforcement gives the all-clear.
FIGHT — As a last resort and only when your life is in imminent danger, act with aggression: improvise weapons (fire extinguisher, furniture), commit fully, and aim to incapacitate.
When law enforcement arrives: keep hands empty and visible, follow all instructions, avoid sudden movements, and do not run toward officers.
avoid: Do not pull fire alarms — this moves people into hallways and can expose them to greater danger.
Do not cluster in groups in open spaces.
Do not attempt to confront or negotiate with a threat unless FIGHT is your only option.
Do not use a hiding spot where you may be trapped with no alternate exit if the threat enters.
Do not assume the situation is over because shooting has stopped — wait for official all-clear.
Do not use elevators during evacuation.

protocols.title: Wildfire
steps: Evacuate immediately when authorities issue an evacuation order or warning — wildfires can change direction and speed without warning.
Before leaving (only if time safely allows): close all windows, doors, vents, and fireplace dampers; shut off gas; leave exterior lights on so your home is visible in smoke.
Take your emergency go-bag: documents, medications, water, phone charger, N95 masks, and clothing that covers all skin.
Drive with headlights on and windows closed; monitor official radio for route guidance and road closures.
If caught in a vehicle: pull off the road, turn on hazards and headlights, stay inside below window level with engine off but blower fan on recirculate setting, cover yourself with a wool blanket.
If on foot and escape is impossible: find a depression or cleared area, lie face down with feet toward the fire, cover your body, and breathe through a cloth or N95 mask close to the ground.
After the fire passes your location: check for smoldering embers on clothing and body; move to a clear area and seek medical attention.
Do not return home until authorities declare the area safe — spot fires and hot spots persist for days.
avoid: Do not shelter in a structure in the fire's direct path — most structures are not rated to withstand direct flame impingement.
Do not drive into smoke — zero-visibility conditions cause accidents and can trap you in the fire's path.
Do not ignore evacuation warnings at 'Watch' level, waiting for 'Warning/Order' — roads clog quickly.
Do not park under trees or power lines during evacuation.
Do not re-enter a burned area while embers are still hot — structural collapse and carbon monoxide are ongoing hazards.
Do not use synthetic clothing — it melts and causes severe burns.

protocols.title: Tornado
steps: Go immediately to your designated shelter: the lowest floor of a sturdy building, in an interior room or hallway away from windows (a bathroom over a basement drain, or a closet under stairs).
If in a basement, shelter under a heavy workbench or staircase and cover your head with a mattress, heavy coat, or hands.
If on an upper floor with no basement, move to the interior-most room on the lowest floor — a bathroom with no exterior walls is ideal.
Crouch low, face down, protecting your neck and head with your arms.
If outdoors and no shelter is reachable: abandon the vehicle, move to the nearest ditch or low-lying ground, lie flat face down with hands covering your head — do not shelter under a bridge.
Stay in shelter until the all-clear is issued by emergency services — the tornado may change direction or a second funnel may follow.
After the storm: check for injuries, watch for downed power lines, gas leaks, and structural damage before re-entering any building.
avoid: Do not attempt to outrun a tornado in a vehicle — vehicles offer almost no protection and can be rolled or carried.
Do not shelter under highway overpasses or bridges — wind speeds are higher in these tunnels.
Do not stay near windows, glass doors, or exterior walls.
Do not use elevators.
Do not go outdoors immediately after a tornado passes — secondary funnels are common.
Do not use candles or open flames after the storm until you confirm there is no gas leak.

protocols.title: Heatwave
steps: Move to the coolest available environment: air-conditioned spaces (libraries, shopping centers, cooling centers) are safest; if none available, the lowest floor of a building stays coolest.
Drink cool water frequently — do not wait until you are thirsty; aim for at least 250 ml (8 oz) every 20–30 minutes if active or exposed.
Apply cool, wet cloths to skin, especially the neck, armpits, and groin where major blood vessels are close to the surface.
If heat exhaustion is suspected (heavy sweating, cold clammy skin, fast weak pulse, nausea, fainting): move the person to a cool place, loosen clothing, apply cool wet cloths, and sip cool water.

If heat stroke is suspected (hot dry or damp skin, rapid strong pulse, confusion, loss of consciousness): call emergency services immediately and cool the person by immersing in cold water or applying ice packs to armpits, groin, and neck.
Check on vulnerable people (elderly, infants, those with chronic illness) at least twice daily during extreme heat.
Close window blinds and curtains on sun-facing sides of the building during the day; ventilate at night when outdoor temperature drops below indoor temperature.
Avoid strenuous outdoor activity between 10:00 and 16:00 local time when solar radiation and temperatures peak.
avoid: Do not leave children, elderly, or pets in parked vehicles even briefly — interior temperatures can exceed 55°C (130°F) within minutes.
Do not consume alcohol or caffeinated beverages as primary hydration — they accelerate dehydration.
Do not rely solely on fans above 35°C (95°F) ambient temperature — fans circulate hot air and provide minimal cooling at these temperatures.
Do not ignore early warning symptoms (dizziness, headache, dark urine) — they precede heat stroke.
Do not eat large, protein-heavy meals that increase metabolic heat production.
Do not reduce water intake if you are not sweating — you may still be losing fluid through respiration.

protocols.title: Gas Leak
steps: If you smell rotten eggs or sulfur (mercaptan odor added to natural gas), or hear a hissing sound near a gas appliance or pipe: do not ignore it — act immediately.
Do not operate any electrical switches, light switches, or electronic devices — even a spark from a switch can ignite gas.
Extinguish all open flames (candles, stoves, fireplaces) only if you can do so without using electrical ignition.
Alert all occupants to evacuate immediately — leave doors and windows open as you exit to allow gas to disperse, but do not delay departure to open them.
Leave the building and move at least 100 metres (330 ft) upwind from the suspected leak site.
Call your gas utility emergency line and emergency services (911 / local number) from outside the building or a safe distance away.
Do not re-enter the building until the utility company and emergency responders declare it safe.
If someone has been overcome by gas inhalation: move them to fresh air, call emergency services, and be prepared to perform CPR if they are unresponsive and not breathing.
avoid: Do not turn any electrical switches on or off — including light switches, garage door openers, or appliances.
Do not use a cell phone, landline, or doorbell inside the building — use them only after you are outside and at a safe distance.
Do not attempt to locate or repair the leak yourself.
Do not start a vehicle in an attached garage — exhaust and ignition sparks are hazardous.
Do not re-enter for any reason until cleared by the utility company.
Do not assume the smell disappearing means the leak has stopped — gas may still be present in unventilated pockets.
